CURRENT AYTAIRS.

When the United States have to stand the expense
of raising their own population the shoe will pinca. Of
many of the exceptional and temporary advantages that
the U.S.A. possess, the obtaining of millions of full-
grown men and women, free, gratis and for nothing, is
Dy no means the least. And Ireland has reared mil-
lions of full-grown persons for America and exported
them to her for nothing. One of the plain tangible
advantages of Home Rule will be the stopping of un-
natural emigration—the unnatural export of valuable
and costly material to another country for no material
consideration. We have estimated the cost of rearing
o full-grown person, over and above the value of any

geryices he or she may have been able to render the

S

counbry before emigration, at £200 a head. It is only
an approximation, and is probably too low. To the
cost of rearing, say, 80,000 full-grown emigrants should
be added the cost of rearing those who die before they
become full grown, for in the rearing of any given
number of people to full growth there is inevitably the
wastage of those who die young. Now Ireland, if we
take the average emigration at 80,000 a year, has the
expense of rearing all those to full age, and the expense
of the proportionate amount of, if we may put 1t so,
breakages. A country like the U.S.A. gets these for
nothing, and she gets similar consignments for nothing
from other countries. The financial drain on Ireland:
owing to this emigration places even our vast over-
taxation in comparative obscurity, for at one time we
were exporting full-grown emigrants for nothing by the:
hundred thousand. Supposing we only take it that
Home Rule will reduce our emigration by 20,000 a year,
that reduction at £200 per head can be expressed im
money terms as a saving to Ireland of no less than
£4,000,000 per annum. And then there is our idea,
which we consider a good one, of putting a tax on emi-
grants, so that those who wish to leave the country
that had the expense of raising them, will at least leave
something to the country for their keep. We would not
suggest that tax in order to artificially stop emigration,
but wholly to raise revenue in a sane and legitimate
way. Ten thousand emigrants paying a tax of £50 a
head—whether the emigrant paid it or the country like:
Canada or Australia that wanted the population would
not affect the result—would yield an annual revenue of
£500,000.

The Young Unionists Branch is the latest political
institution in Ireland—and we may hereinafter refer to
them as the Yubs for short. Now we have the Yibs
and the Yubs. So far as the names of those present
at the opening meeting of the Yubs, and so far as we
know anything of the persons the names stand for, are
concerned, the chief thing to be said of the Yubs is that
they are pretty old fogies. Where are the cubs of the
Yubs? for, judging by the names given in the *‘ Irish
Times,"’ those present appeared to be all old foxes.

We glanced through the speech of Mr. J. G. Butcher,

M.P., and it was poor, raucous stuff. . Poor Sir Horace

Plunkett is now being used as a good second to ‘‘Molly
Maguire ”’ in the anti-Irish campaign. Mr. Butcher,
for the purposes of his anti-Irish harangue, referred to.
poor Sir Horace as ‘ one of the greatest and most unsel-
fish Trishmen that ever lived.””  Poor Sir Horace must
be weak indeed when he has not strength enough to ask
these anti-Trish spouters to shut up for decency salke.

Brother Campbell, K.C., the notorious Freemason,
was another speaker to the Yubs, and he gave out
Billingsgate and Dan, Dan, Danny McCann in his usuak
style. He made a very bad and laughable blunder in
his perfervid peroration. He evidently intended to
evoke the thunderous cheers of the Yubs by something
in the ‘‘ once on board the lugger the ship is mine ’”
style. What he said was:—“ And that he, for one,
would never hesitate, either in public or in private, to
urge and persuade his loyalist countrymen to resort to
every means, every means available to men of honour.
and courage, before they would consent to be deprived
of that which was their heritage—the right to be
governed by laws that received the sanction of the
Imperial Parliament.” The laws of an Irish Parlia-
ment will fully answer Brother Campbell’s definifion,

for the Irish Parliament will be set up by a law passed
by tHe Imperial Parliament—by the King, Lords and
Commons.

The Irish agricultural labourer, when he has a house
and a plot ought to be one of the most independent
men in the land. The measure of his prosperity no
doubt depends much on the use he malkes of his plot.
The Department has published, at the price of one
penny, an informing pamphlet on ¢ The Management
of a Cottage Garden’ that ought to have a wide circu-
lation. This is one sort of reading that is much needed
in this alleged agricultural country. ~ Mr. T. P. Gill
eontributes an excellent preface to the pamphlet, so
that the reader gets a delightful essay as well as prac-
tical instruction for his penny. Mr. T. P. Gill, so far
as we can judge, was the possessor of the brain out of
which the Department emerged, but being a mere Irish
Tipperary man, it is not, shall we say, policy, to give
him the credit. It is for the mere Irish Tipperaryman
to appear as if he walked behind whilst others who
are neither mere Irish nor from Tipperary are to he
regarded by all tame and ‘‘ respectable’” people as the
men who carry the flag. Having no prejudices against
a mere Irish Tipperaryman ourselves, we have no
temptation against giving credit where credit happens
o be earned by a man from that county. The Trish
agricultural labourer when he has a cottage and a plct
is & man to be envied. As Mr. Gill says:—‘‘ The
cottage and the acre or half-acre plot, let it be recalled,
are usually provided at a rent (on an average about a
shilling to one and sixpence a week) amounting to less
than it costs the district council to maintain them.
In contemplating this paternal system, and the country
labourer in the midst of it, living his wholesome open-
air life, his varied, developed life, one thinks of the
contrast with the worker in the typical city tenement,
with his soulless toil and sordid surroundings, to whom
even personal cleanliness is often almost impossible
because of the squalor that overwhelms him.”’

Further on Mr. Gill says:—*‘ This is not the place to
discuss the various aspects of that problem; but it
« comes to this, that the labourer is the pivot of cur
agriculture. His efficiency, his relations with the
farmer, his civil and social status, his education, sre
all vital national questions. The importance of the
subject cannot be exaggerated. And not the least of
the factors in the case is the management of that cot-
tage and plot which is now one of the characteristic
features of an Irish landscape.’

The following, on the labourer with his house and plot,
is well worth quoting :—‘‘ He is no longer either a sort
of serf tied to the holding, or a sort of outcast, nobody
owning to him; he has now his place as defined and
honourable as that of anyone in the community. With
steady employment at a good wage, or, to put it an-
other way about, with the skill and character which
will command the steady employment and the good
wage—our advancing agriculture is crying out for the
labourer with skill and character—his is a favourable
state economically and socially : better, often, than that
of the small farmer, with a holding just beyond the
border line, burdened with his responsibilities.  And
over and above the earning’and the other conditions
the cottage and plot give him one of the first of the
guarantees of happiness, the possibility of a healthy and
even beautiful home life. This factor, I think, cannot

THE LEADER.

JANUARY 18, 1912.

be too much insisted on. No one will think it can who
has been brought into close gontaqb'\_vlth some of the
features of modern industrial civilization, with its
horrors, whether of employment or unemployment in
the life of the urban poor, for whom no such boon is
available. It has not been ingisted on epou.gh. And
the labourer himself has not learned to realise it enough.
A neat cobtage to himself and an ample garden—a placcf
he and his wife can keep, inside and uultsx_de (we lmv.n
all seen them so kept), with exquisite daintiness, ﬂOW:Ol‘-.
clad, tree-shaded, green and fragrant, herbs and fruit
growing around them for their food, the 'houey-hc.a
working for them in her hi God’s pure air for their
breathing, the grass and the dge-row 11174 the stream,
the robin and the lark, their children’s daily company.
The village school is hard by and the village church.
An idylll" Yes, in ov prose reality an idyll the
Trish rustic labourer’s h nay be to-day if he and his
wife only understand what they have in their posses-
sion and only know how to use it. Now all these
agencies (this little pamphlet being one of therp) are ab
hand to help them to know how to use ib.”

Policeman Reynolds, of Carlow, is retiring on pen-
sion and has been appointed court keeper in Carlow.
He is therefore a pensioner and the holder of a paid
job. Where therefore could be found a more fitting
porson for a testimonial? A local Nationalist paper
tells us that *“ he was the most able man that held the
position in Carlow for years.”” The hat, it appears, IS
to be sent round for this police pensioner, but we think
Carlow people who have money to give away could find
better use for it. Is there a St. Vincent de Paul So-
ciety in Carlow ?

THE PERIL OF THE BLACK NORTH.

Scene.—A suburban parleur with two nervous old ladies
taking tea.

1st O. L.—This drilling in the North,
And Orange preparation for the field
Beneath the mask of secrecy and stealth
Doth fill me with deep dread.

2nd O. L.—From camp to camp, through the grim hush
of night,
The hum of dark manceuvres stilly sounds.

1st O. L.—In Ulster when the sun was low,
No caw was heard of carrion crow,
And calm was loyal Sandy Row
And every saved locality.

But Ulster saw another sight,

When Home Rule passed in dead of night,
Commanding rebel fires to light .
Its ominous tranquillity.

With maledictions deep and loud

On Rome and Fngland’s faithless crowd,
Stood Carson’s army, grim and proud
To shake off chains and slavery. -

2nd O. L.—T prithee cease,
And go no further with that picture dread,
Which time T fear will bring to lurid light.

I wish I could believe, as others do,
" 'hat Ulster’s iron is but painted lath
Lo fright and not to fight.

1st O, I..—T have heard it said—
"Tig the Orangeman frights,
And the Papist that smites.
The same is not the truth T greatly dread.

2nd 0. L.—OL, I do fear,
Though empty vessels make the greatest sound,
That Ulster’s war-cloud is an omen dread
That tells of flaming thunderbolts.

1st O. L.—'Tis said each loyal man above the Boyne
Of late doth look most resolute and grim,
Most terrible and fierce.

2nd O. L.—'Tis said he clutches at a pint of beer,
Or glass of whisky, as the case may be,
As if it were a Papist.

1st O. L.—And even bLere I notice changes too.
The lawyer saved doth fiercely clutch his bag
As if he held a sword.

2nd O. I.—The other day I noticed in Rathmines
Two sourfaced Johnnies, like insurance clerks,
Who stood conversing with dark stealthy looks,
And one of them unto the other gave
A sixpence as I passed, and as he did,
He spoke the word remember.

1st O. L.—Ha! He gave that coin, as surely as I live,
To purchase arms.

2nd O. L.—That thought did st~1‘il{o me at the very time.

1st O. L.—'Tis strange no comet in the sky doth bode
A dire disaster in the days to come.

2nd 0. L.—No fiery herald of disaster yet
Hath glared upon us from the midnight sky ;
But some do say that other sights are seen
Most wonderful and strange.

1st O. L.—I prithee tell me of those wondrous things.
2nd 0. L.—In Plunkett House a leprecaun was seen,
Who carried in his hand a bulging purse

That held a prince’s ransom.

1st 0. L.—Did no one collar it !

- 2nd 0. .—A Celtic bard

TUpon the golden goblin got his eye

And held him fixed with avaricious gaze ;

But, as it happens with a fairy e’er,

The moment that the bard put out his hand

To grab the spoil, his eager clutch got hold
-of only empty air. 2

1st O. I,.—That tells of something bad.

2nd 0. I.—There is a rumour, too,
That Yeats hath heayd the banshee wailing loud
About his pension.

18t 0. T..—That settles it.

Ulster will fight. A. M. W.

THE LEADER. 639

The poor *“ Trish Times * is being dragged after the
heels of Carson and is now a prospective rebel: it backs
high treason and has not a word of denunciation for
those who are announcing their intention of taking up
arms against his most gracious majesty George V. 1f
the disaffected people in India and other places come
to hear of the treasonable tall of Carson and the atti-
tude of the * Trish Times,” it may have serious conse-
quences.

These are rather hard times for the * Trish Times =0
staff : they don’t look like heroes, and to give them their
due we don’t suppose the sourest of them wants to pose
us one. It was said to us that every man on the staff
of the ** Trish Times’’ but one is a Home Ruler! Pro-
bably there is some exaggeration in that. But the poor
chaps, even though their lot is not too easy nor their
wage too high,_are not anxious to quit this world any
sooner than they can help, and some of them t}lab we
know by appearance would make cats laugh if they
carried rifles on their shoulders.

One of the staff, whose name we do not know, dresses
like the picture of a conventional 98 insurgent. We
suppose he will lead the dread battalion from West-
moreland Street! According to the ‘‘ Trish Times’
leading article of Saturday, ‘‘ More than a million of
the most industrious and self-respecting citizens. of
Treland will use physical force to prevent Home Rule
from becoming operative in Ulster.”” They might have
said twenty millions as they were about it: why this
cheeseparing of numbers ?

Last week we commented on the terrible Christmas
Bve fatality at Athy, when a man who had been drink-
ing heavily was found dead in a stable. We have re-
ceived a letter from an Athy correspondent, who re-
marks :—* He had been drinking in Athy all day on
Saturday, and the Athy publicans finished him on Sun-
day. Alcoholic poisoning is not mentioned nor are the
publicans censured by the Coroner, who seems to have
been rather jocose.”” There were two Bungs on the
jury. Our correspondent goes on:—‘‘ We are wallow-
ing in drink in Athy, and there does not seem to be even
a remote prospect of our improving. We guard our
publicans with great care, we take a great interest in
their business and would not: for the world lessen it in
any way. The police, too, have the utmost regard for
the publicans; their interests must be safeguarded at
all costs, aye, even though they degrade or slay half
the population. As to temperance or total abstinence,
no one would be heard advocating such things openly
in Athy. We did great business in the drunkeries
about Christmas and the New Year: the Sundays espe-
cially were great drinking days with us.”” This is a
strong indictment of Athy. Has Athy any reply to
male to it ? 5

It is hard to be surprised at any act of bigotry that
happens in Rathmines. The Rathmines Urban Council
is a preserve for non-Catholics, and nearly all the paid
jobs—if not all—worth having are held by non-Catho-
lics. Of course some of the hewers of wood and drawers
of water in the employment of the bigoted and intole-
rant Rathmines Council are *‘ Idolaters.” TLast Satur-
day was a holiday of the Church, and twenty Catholic
men in the employment of the Urban Council were
prevented from attending Mass. They were about to
go to Mass as usual when they were ordered to go to

part of the previous record of intolerance by the Rath-

. mines Urban Council, for this was the first occasion

that these men were not allowed to go to Mass on &
Catholic' holiday.  The overseer of these men is an
English importation and is in the employment of the
Commissioners for several years. This latest piece of
virulent bigotry has caused great indignation and dis-
gust amongst the men concerned, who are a respect-
able, temperate, honest body of men. Are the Catho-
lics of Rathmines so tame that they will put up with
this without a fight? Ts this a sample of Continental
atheistic tactics against Catholics.  If this attempt
succeeds without rousing the righteous indignation of
the Catholic ratepayers of Rathmines, we suppose even
further acts of like bigotry and intolerance may be ex-
pected in the future. It will be remembered that it
was an employe of the Rathmines Commissioners who
was guilty of the unspeakable sacrilegeous outrage at
the Earlsfort Rink. Now we have twenty honest Catho-
lic workingmen not allowed to go to Mass on a Catholic
holiday. Considering that the Catholic population of
the Urban District is about equal to, if not in excess of,
the non-Catholic population, all Ireland must cry shame
on Rathmines Catholics if they take this act of intoler-
ance lying down.

The special correspondent of the London ‘‘ Times "
has discovered the Sourfaces. He saw them at Omagh
going to hear Cawson. He announces his discovery in
these words:—"“As I watched them file past in dis-
ciplined array and studied the hard angular faces . . .
affording such a marked contrast to the softer and
reunder features of the Celtic South. i

Here is a light on the early breaking in of West
Britons in Ireland. Carson at Omagh said:—‘T re-
member in my boyhood days, almost in my bringing-
up, when I was first taught to say my prayers at my
mother’s knee, I was taught to think of England as a
country that loved liberty, that loved religion, and that
loved justice, and I believed it, and I believe it still.”

The nearer the church the farther from God. Who
would ever think that a bungery in Maynooth would
permit drunkenness on its premises? and run by a lady,
too. A man named Dunne was found sitting in a chair
drunk on the premises on November 30th. ~It appears
that the man Dunne was a wreck from drink. Bung’s
bond slave swore that the man was not supplied with
any drink, but the owner of the bungery was fined 20s.
and costs for permitting a drunken man to be on the
‘premises.

The village of Dysart contains three houses and the
«adult population is,only five: the total population is
fourteen. Of the three houses that comprise the vil-
lage of Dysart two are bungeries, and there was an
application at the Roscommon Quarter Sessions re-
specting a licence for the third! The three houses thab
‘comprise the village of Dysart, with its five adults and
fourteen human beings all told, were all bungeries until
the licence of the house that was the subject of the
application at Quarter Sessions lapsed or atherwise be-
came non est. The parish priest, Father Coleman,
opposed the turning of Dysart into a cent. per cent.
bungery encampment. ‘‘Time after time,”” said the
parish priest, ‘‘ wives and mothers come to me to com-
plain of their husbands and sons remaining out in these
houses, coming home drunk and beating them. Tt is a
sad case. We have some of these wives waiting up
antil 11 and 12 at night, waiting for drunken husbands
to come in, and when they do come in they beat their

“A million and & half of the most progressive ‘and
prosperous citizens of Ireland " is one of the latest
political batons of the ** Irish Times. Most progres-
sive and prosperous indeed!  Picture the hordes of
submerged, hard-drinking and ignorant Orangemen in
the front row of ** the most progressive and prosperous
citizens "’| And then there are those of the sweated
workers of Belfast, who also are pillars of the Umoxv:
and in the figating line of the *“ to hell with Popery
brigade. A group of these sweated ones would look
picturesque in a procession of ‘‘ the most progressive
and prosperous citizens "’ |

A correspondent writes to inform us that Brother
Alton, Chief Inspector of the ‘‘ National ”” Bank—of
whom we had something to say in recent issues—is a
near relative-in-law to the ‘‘ saved ’’ Mr. Healy, who
edits the anti-Irish *‘ Irish Times.”” Brother Alton
has great influence in the now so-called ™ National ™
Bank, particularly since the death of the Jate Mr. Slat-
tery. The majority of the officials of this bank are
Catholics, but Brother Alton is a member of the secret
and pernicious society of the Free and Accepted. This
bank was founded by O’Connell as a patriotic institu-
tion : now it has its head office in London and the chief
confidential adviser of its directors is this arch-Free-
mason, Brother Alton. 3

That home of genius in Stephen’s Green, the
“ National ’ Literary Society, held its annual conver-
sazione—no shorter word would do them to describe it.
There was not as much as one song in Irish at this
“ National ’’ conversazione: the first song was ‘‘ The
Green Isle of Erin.” It is a green isle indeed when
things like this conversazione are held in it under the
auspices of an alleged ‘‘ National '’ Literary Society.
The last song on the programme was ‘‘ Di Provenza.”’
The National Literary Society should hold its next con-
versazione in Rathmines—it would be a most suitable
venue.

Cannot the Women's National Health Society get on
without the aid of bung? At Tralee Petty Sessions
recently an application was made for an occasional
license from 9.30 p.m. to 4 a.m. in connection with the
dance in aid of the Women’s National Health Society.
The application was granted. What does Lady Aber-
deen think of this? We think it was of Tralee it was
said that you couldn’t fall in the streets without falling
into a publichouse.  Yet in that town the Women’s
Health people cannot have a dance without adding to
it a temporary bungery until 4 o’clock a.m.

There was a good national spirit amongst the lads of
University College about the time the LiEADER started.
The Irish Ireland enthusiasm caught them. That layer
of students—if we may use that expression—h:\\"ing
passed out into the world, the succeeding layers of Uni-
versity students would appear to have grown less' and
less Trish, less and less lads of ideals and spivit.  Is
there any National spivit in the students of tho Dublin
College of phc National University to-day? We sup-
pose thc;re is some, but, so far as the public receive any
impression, there might be very little. And, after all,
the students of the Dublin College of the National Uni-
versity ought to have something positive in them so
that the public could not be allowed to forget they exist.
Do they believe in anything in the vigorous, impulsive

Youth ? . Do they believe in Irish Ireland, or are. they

Opinion on a publication that we only sampled, we must

A correspondent sends us a copy of a threepenny
English weekly of which we never heard before. It is
a thing called ** The Throne.”” It appears to be'a sort
of jingo and anti-Irish paper. Our correspondent directs
our attention to an drticle, or notes, that show to us
n‘hat poor, ignorant fools the readers of such papers as

The Throne ”” must be. There is a lot of silly stuft
about the ‘‘ Celts,”’ whoever they are, but as nothing
-good is said of them we are not surprised that the article
in this English jingo journal says that ‘' the purest
Celtic -blood is found in Wales and Ireland.” We
suppose Wales is put in as a backhander to Lloyd-
George. Here is the sort of thing that apparently goes
down with the Iinglish readers of the threepenny
‘* Throne *’ :—

The celt to-day is what he always was : sallow, blackhalred,
undersized, revengeful, merciless and priest-ridden.

And again:—

To the carliest investigators the Irish were famed for three
qualities. They were always quarrelling, they were invariably
priest-ridden, and they were cannibals. If they cannot now
practice the last they make amends by the latitude they permit
themselves in regard to the two former. And it is these people
who are allowed to dictate to their conquerors what the govern-
ment of the day should be! They who were treated as a race
only fit to be slaves and who were driven out by their betters from

A LITTLE IN PRAISE OF OURSELVES.

T HE LeADER is quite an old-established institution

in the land. Its circulation is very considerably
greater to-day than it was this time twelve months, and
we are by no means satisfied that we have reached any-
tlfing like a limit. We hope to increase it further dur-
ing this year. It certainly did not thrive by fawning
on people anyway, and we suppose it is no exaggeration
to say that no paper in Iréland has more bitter enemies
or more genuine friends. A simple man wrote to ‘us
this week stating that it might be useful to us to lknow
some of the things that are said against us! We thinlk
we are fairly familiar with them, and we never feel
stronger than when the abusive letters fall thick upon
us. Our innocent correspondent told us that one of his
acquaintances didn’t like the LEADER *‘ because he was
always attacking someone.’”” Did you ever?  And
what in the name of common-sense is a part, and a per-
manent part, of the Leaper’s work but to be always
attacking someone: a more legitimate complaint would
be that we don’t attack enough.

Though we are told plenty of unpleasant things, no
doubt for our own good, and get our share of abusive
lefters and so forth, we also receive considerable evi-
dence on the other side. i

. His lordship the Most Rev. Dr. Fogarty, Bishop of

of education, however painful to the scholar. How I
wish to see it read universally by the young men of
Ireland. The ¢ Penny Dirty’ and ‘ Halfpenny Shocker’
are becoming a national peril. The worst thing, I be-
lieve, that could happen a nation is to have its ordinary
reading public fed from the pages of a Press that has

abundant supply of such journalism. I regard the

learned man is a stimulus to us at the commencement
of the year’s work. There is nothing namby-pamby,
nor goody-goody, about the Leaber. It is a strong

is mainly Catholic—run by one of the majority, who is
no theologian, but stands up for tolerance, justice, and
fair play for Catholics in secular life, and stands up for
the interests of the Irish nation. We are encouraged
by the large number of disinterested {riends the LEADER
has made. It is a paper that appeals to people witn
brains, with a sense of humour and with a serious and
earnest outlook on life, particularly on Irish life. Though

N replying to *‘ Piran’s’’ article in last week’s
LEADER, I must first of all thank him for the
thoroughly admirable spirit in which he has taken up
my contentions, and I can only trust that in answering
him I will maintain the same standard of reasoning.
I must thank him also for interesting himself in this
question of Irish games, though indeed our views on
many points may not be identical, nevertheless a little
discussion is one of the best things for bringing out the

most favourable arguments for your case.

I can assure ‘‘ Piran” that T would never have at-
tempted to criticise Rugby if I had not a very good
experience of it. My experiences of it are all of what
““ Piran ’ speaks—those of the scrum, and such were
they that at one time I made for my motto the words
of Mangan :—

*“ Woe and pain, pain and woe,
Are my lot night and noon!”’
And indeed I fear I have not very much to show for
my exertions. Six or seven years of scrummaging have
left me with—well, T admit that my shoulders may not
be too bad, but I must confess that my wrists and arms

MUSEUMS.

]':‘ROM the practice of going to museums to look ab

objects, artistic and historical, many people
would seem to have come to believe that such objects
are intended to be shut up in museums, either private
or public. And these good people are encouraged in
this belief. ~ The museum committees and curators
encourage the belief by their incessant eagerness to
enrich the collections already in their charge. These
committees will hunt, and strive, and argue until they
have got the object upon which they have set their eyes
or hearfs. Of course the notion is wrong. That it is
wrong is so plain as hardly to require statement. It
would be as true to believe that animals and plants are
intended for zoological gardens and botanical collec-
tions. -
Objects of art, for example, are intended for furniture
and dress, for the adornment of domestic or public
walls and public places. If through unavoidable
occurrences these objects must be removed from the
places for which they were designed, and they have no
longer a right to exist, museums might be provided to
shelter them and save them from extinction. In such
places these objects are as man in the grave. Their
work is done, they no longer serve the purposes for
which they were intended.  But to tear them from
their places merely for the purpose of herding them in
museums and collections is as if a living man were
taken to be put into a tomb. It is murder.

Public opinion was outraged in France some few
years ago, even in the midst of the anti-clerical agita-
tion, by & proposal from the Government to take the
art treasures from the churches and place them in the -
museums.  The reliquaries and statues were to be
taken from the churches where they were eloquent and
be imprisoned in the museums where they would be
mute for ever. Not alone mute but incongruous. The
statues and other: religious objects in a church are
usually in harmony with its architecture and decora-
tion; in a museum they would stand alone, harmonis-
ing not with its surroundings nor having any relations
with them. They might, perhaps, retain some value
from the fact of fine workmanship, bub that would be
all. Torn from their proper places and standing on
their pedestals around the walls of a circular court, as

In England some people have got this museum craze
rather badly. They want national museums, county
museums, town museums, even village museums.
These things may be all right in their own way and in
their own place; but they are not permitted to remain
in either. The latest turn this craze has taken is that
London should have what they are pleased to call a
Follt Museum. What that means is doubtful; but as
usual the leading spirvit in the scheme is a museum
official. - Dr. Bather, of the South Kensington Museum,
is that leading spivit, and he is quite ecstatic over the
idea. ‘“ The fundamental idea of such a scheme,’” he
says, ““is to establish a museum which would be full
of living interest and link up the past by imperceptible
stages with the present.”” Just what all such institu-
tions want to do. In this museum the doctor would
put under cover illustrations of the steady development
of the arts and crafts. ‘‘ Rural industries which still
exist but ave in danger of extinction might be practised
here as in a permanent on.”” So you see his
museum will have a ‘‘living '’ interest, for will it not
Le a work place for what ave generally called, here at
least, the ‘‘ Home Industries?”” He wants, too, to
preserve other things. ** Farm buildings, church bel-
fries, mills, maypoles, round towers and ancient land-
marks might find in an open-air museum a suitable
Lhome. Primitive houses might be filled with the furni-
ture appropriate to their period, and stretches of water
give ample scope for exhibiting the development of
boat-building in its appropriate setting.”” There is the
museum maniac in all his glo Mills, church towers,
and round towers which have stood the test of ages in
their original positions are to be ‘‘ preserved ’ in this
great Folk Museum. An Trish museum to hold all the
ruinous church towers, mills and round towers would
need to be rather extensive. But why put such things
in a museum at all? Imagine Glendalough in a great
Folk Museum of the future, and the Giant’s Cause-
way. And farm buildings, too, as if these things could
not be preserved, and retain more interest, in the places
where they were built. These buildings are erected in
accordance with local conditions, and would probably
lose any little interest they have when taken from the
locality. But such a comprehensive museum. Every-
thing, from primitive houses, preserved, of course, with
appropriate furniture, to living people practising dead
or dying crafts, is to find space therein. He has put
his eyes on London’s Crystal Palace as the ‘* preserv-
ing ” place for this comprehensive and motley collec-
tion. The learned doctor, taking it for granted that
he is learned, thinks evidently that things can be pre-
served better in the Crystal Palace in London than in
the places where they have stood for centuries. He
forgets that a thing possesses its full value only in the
place it ought to be. Anywhere else its meaning is

lost.

said, *‘ Deutschland tiber Alles ” (Germans over all)
had taken its place. No more need of that centuried
watch by the Rhine, the warlike appeal had given
place to a proud assurance against the world. No
sneers ab his country, neither was there undue bragging ;
and it was pleasant to watch the quiet ineffable pride
with which in the course of conversation he threw bacl
his head and said, “ You know we are all soldiers in
Germany.” And, stranger still, in conversing some
time ago with a supposed Englishman, he quietly cor-
rected me and said he was not Inglish, he belonged
to a race that had conquered England. Te was from
the Channel Islands, and his Norman blood was too
proud to claim kinship with the neighbouring island
whose flag floats over one-quarter of this globe. The
FEnglishman, the German, the Frenchman, the obscure
Channel Tslander—each proud of his country, and the
Trishman 2—What is it? Is it modesty? Is it sla-
very ? TLong despised by the Saxon, have we at long
last reached that awful depth when we begin to despise
ourselves ? It looks somewhat like it. We discarded
our language in our ignorance, and in spite of the
taelic Teague and other agencies the process is still
going on, and we seem bent on discarding everythi
that would link us with the past and keep us distine-
tive as a nation.

It we had chosen to imitate the virile ngland of
Tlizabeth, the I'rance of Touis XIV., or the Germany
of the present, something could be said for that atti-
tude; but to turn to an effete and rotten empive for our
model is a state of things as deplorable as it is inexplic-
able. I do think this English dominion has got a

tighter grip on our souls than we sometimes think; and
I believe further that at no period in our history have
our intellectual qualities been ab a lower ebb, thanks
chiefly to the tons of inane English literature that are
daily dumped on our shores. I hear men speak with
good-natured amusement of our simple unlettered
grandfathers, with that superior air with which ignor-
ance always treats a past generation ; and it seems to me
the balance is in favour of the men of the olden time.
At their meetings in the long winter evenings they dis-
cussed their fairy lore, the Gaelic traditions that lin-
gered still with the olden speech.  Superstition, you
will say; yes, perhaps, but something of an intellectual
and spiritual Havour at any rate. Go into a company
of young men at the present day and what do you often
find with all their supposed advantages of education ?
Cheap sneers, shallow scepticism, coarse jokes, and a
wit that scarcely ever reaches the level of decent buf-
foonery. Put forward the proposition that there are
men who do a day’s work for Ireland without thought
of personal aggrandisement and you get properly
laughed  at. Has this nation sunk so low at last that
the spirit of self-sacrifice has ceased to be intelligible
to the common mind. Shades of Emmet and Thomas
Street of 1803 !

We read some few years ago with a thrill of pride
(and with a thuill of envy, thinking the while of Ire-
land), of a fourteen year old boy, the only son of a Boer
General, wlio rode into action by his father’s side, and
died; but then—they did not have a long night of
slavery behind to sap the national virtues. It seems
to me that we are suffering from the effects of half
education, which is in many respects worse than ignor-
ance. We see that our flag has gone down in a defeat
that is monotonous through all the centuries, but we
forget that considering the relative strength and re-
sources of the neighbouring islands it was almost
mathematically certain that it should be so. As the
doggerel rhyme well expresses it :—

‘“ Her soldiers are many, abroad and at home,
Her ships on all oceans are ploughing the foam,
Her wealth is untold. Sure no equal was she
For my poor plundered Erin afar o’er the sea!’

And we forget at the same time that her Wild Geese

carried her flag in a blaze of glory through Europe.

In our worship of success we fail to see that the glory

is not always, with the conquering hosts, that the blood

spilled in the Coliseum triumphed finally, and the
glory and the power of Rome passed away. I do not
say that we should wilfully shut our eyes to our faults,
and, ostrich-like, stick our heads in the sand, b\lq i dp
say unless we nourish a proper and legitimate pride in
our country and its past, I'see no hope for the future.

T care not for tall chimney stacks covering the land

and cities of pallid faces; they may come : but no nation

- THE LEADER. R 549

ltiuilb on a foundation of materialism alone ever yet en-
ured.

But the most inexplicable thing I notice in this
latter-day Ireland is the scoffing tone you so frequently
hear concerning religious matters which remind one in
a distant way of France. If a man choose to be a
libertine, a disbeliever (or mock disbeliever) in Revela-
tion, he has the gift of free-will and may, select his
road ; but why not keep it to himself, why spit it out
in railway carriages and public places, in- presence of
unformed minds, and worse, the curiously plastic. mind
of youth? I have met many well-meaning men who
unconsciously sin in this respect, but it 1s degrading,
disgusting, and unmanly. Are the penal days a myth ?
Is it true that our fathers sacrificed all rather than
conform to the creed of the conqueror? We prized
the old creed when it hid away in bogs and mountains,
and if our talk be a trustworthy criterion, we value it
much less when it walks abroad untrammelled in all
its majesty. If this creed be not a thing of beauty,
a great and crowning glory of the race, we ought to have
relinquished it some centuries ago when we could have
exchanged our rags for the silks of Mammon.. That
spinal column of ours needs watchful attention by the
physician or we shall be soon loving all we ought to
hate and despising all we ought to prize. ~We were
ashamed of our language till the Gaelic League came
on the scene, and it looks as if some of our people are
getting educated enough to be ashamed of their fathers’
creed, and to think it necessary to make semi-apologies
or it in presence of other denominations. A young
ady told me a few weeks ago that she attended a
Catholic Church in Germany in the early autumn of the
past year and could not find standing room, and a few
Sundays later did not have two dozen fellow-wor-
shippers in the Madeleine at Paris. Does this account
or the growing grandeur of Germany and the deca-
dence of France? I have never read of a nation great
in history that did not have its foundation in religion.
The remains of Roman temples are standing still, Japan
is a land of temples, and England was great in her
Catholic days and great under Puritan Cromwell.

There are many other curious traits in the present-
day Irish mind which are obvious enough to anyone
who takes the slightest trouble to observe (and to which
I may revert); still it is an easy matter to criticise, and
I do not wish these few remarks to bear a pessimistic
tone. T have a full and abiding faith in the future of
this country (that we have survived as a nation is
something of a miracle), and I believe her Easter morn
is very close at hand.

purpose in this article considering the question of Irish

port.

knowledge of the points at issue. I write with a desire
to help and not to criti In my former articles on
Home Rule I dealt with the question of Irish repre-
sentation at Westminster and outlined the possible
methods of solution, expressing a preference on grounds
of expediency for the Gladstonian solution of 1893,

Home Rule. In the autumn of 1910 a considerable
portion of the English Unionist Press vaguely advo-

ing over the tasty words, ‘‘ Federal Home Rule,’’ in
their minds, whilst occasionally unthinking people of

In the last analysis it is an argument not only from
the Union bub for the Union. At the same time it
furnishes a neat example of the peculiar political power
of a phrase. The whole thing is a metaphor and it is
just the metaphor that appeals to the imagination.
Similarly we might argue that from the Union of Great
Britain and Treland had been born the many daughters
of the Impire family and that a divorce (i.c., Home
Rule) between the ‘* parent stocks’” would necessarily
bring ruin and disaster to the daughter colonies. Yet
this is obviously absurd. As a fact, Irishmen were
builders of the FEmpire: qua Trishmen severally and
individually, but Ireland qua Ireland did not help to
make the Empire, it is a thorn in the Empire’s side
and will so continue till Home Rule is won. In any
case Ireland is not in the least bit anxious to put in a
claim for the excrcise of parental rights, either in the
Sritish Bmpire or the United States, of which, too, it
is the *‘ parent stock.”
The inclusion of Irish members at Westminster will
give the Unionist Party a far stronger argument against
the Bill than their total exclusion. The dominating
feeling in Tingland is to give us Home Rule in order to
get rid of the Irish question and the Irish representa-
tives at Westminster and to leave Parliament free to
deal with Tmperial and English questions. The argu-

-ment has been tersely put by Mr. Broadbent’s valet,

Hodson, in ““ John Bull’s Other Island.”

“I'm an Owm Ruler, I am, do you know why? It’s
because I want a little attention paid to my own coun-
try; and that'll never be as long as your chaps are
‘ollerin’ at Westminster as if nowbody mettered but
your own bloomin’ selves. Send ’em back to Hell or

sick of Ireland. Let jt gow. Cut the cable. Make it a

while; and give poor owld England a chance; that’s

Unionists will rise this argument against retention.
of Irish members at Westminster. They will also say
as Mr. Balfour said in 1893 :—‘‘ Those questions (of
representation in the English Parliament) are incapable
of solution, and the very fact that they are incapable of
solution affords, in our opinion, a conclusive argument
against the whole scheme, of which one or other of the
plans in question must form a part.”” Mr. Balfour was
right when he said that the question of representation
at Westminster was difficult to solve, he was quite
wrong when he said that such representation neces-
sarily formed a part of a Home Rule scheme. It does
not. The solution by way of non-representation is
easy, statesmanlike, and sensible. The question can-
not be tinkered with ; it must be faced boldly and treated

the majority of our pupils. 4

I have dealt thus fully with the examination in
T'rench not so much on account of its own importance
as with the object of showing that the Commissioners,
who are mainly amateurs, busy with their own several
callings, fail to do their duty as Commissioners of Edu-
cation either through ignorance or lack of time; that
consequently a radical change is required in the com-
position of the Intermediate Board; that new blood
should be infused into this important body by the in-
clusion of one or two members representative of the
interests of the head masters and one or two represen-
tative of the assistant masters. But in dealing at
length with the examination in French I have the fur-
ther and more important object of impressing on the
public the necessity of their making themselves
acquainted with education of all sorts, from the kinder-
garten to the University, if they are interested in Ire-
land’s progress. A sound knowledge of the trend of
education is of primary importance for parents and
guardians, and. in order that they may get the best
possible one for those placed under their care they must
interest themselves in everything connected with it—
programmes, teachers, inspectors, examiners. When
they know what modern requirements demand they
must see that they get it from all who are responsible
for them. This they can in many cases most effec-
tively do by bringing, both individually and collectively,
pressure to bear on the M.P.’s so that they in their turn
will be alive to the importance of all forms of sound
education.  One result that would spring from this at-
tention on the part of the M.P.’s would be that we
should be spared the scandal of finding Irish Inter-
mediate education done out of about £60,000 for the
past six or eight years owing to the neglect of this im-
portant subject. Again, we should be spared the in-
caleulable loss to the nation of having the rising gene-
ml,im_l of Trish men and women taught by teachers
deficient in learning, untrained and underpaid ; and we
should finally be spared the wortification of having still
in Treland a system of secondary education as unsound
as it is unprogressive, and which has been condemned
by all authorities. T.ef us remember that it is worthy

of an aspiring and intellectual race like the Trish to aim -

ab and procure whatever is best and highest in educa-
tion. BXPERT. *
